did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a machine learning approach for skin disease identification. Specifically, it employed Gray Level Co-Occurrence Matrix (GLCM) for feature extraction (Contrast and Energy) and the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) classifier to classify skin diseases (Acne and Psoriasis) from captured images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a skin disease identification system using a Raspberry Pi, USB camera, GLCM feature extraction, and k-NN classification. It includes a methodology section, hardware block diagrams, a conceptual framework, and reports specific experimental results (100% accuracy for Acne and 92% for Psoriasis from 25 trials each). The title and content indicate it is a technical study/prototype implementation rather than a review of existing literature.

title: Skin Disease Identification System using Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix
brief_summary: This study developed a skin disease identification system using a Raspberry Pi, USB camera, and LCD touchscreen to classify Acne and Psoriasis. The system utilized Gray Level Co-Occurrence Matrix (GLCM) features, specifically Contrast and Energy, extracted via wavelet decomposition for normalization. Training data was sourced from DermNet and documented skin disease images to train a k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) classifier. The device was evaluated through 25 trials per disease, with accuracy assessed using T-Test analysis. The system achieved 100% accuracy for Acne and 92% accuracy for Psoriasis. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed prototype for accessible skin disease diagnosis without laboratory screenings.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary skin diseases classified by the system. Tested with 25 trials, achieving 100% accuracy.
One of the two primary skin diseases classified by the system. Tested with 25 trials, achieving 92% accuracy.

all_task_types.details: The paper describes a skin disease identification system that classifies skin images into discrete categories: Acne and Psoriasis. The system uses a k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) classifier to predict these disease labels based on GLCM features (Contrast and Energy). The results report accuracy percentages (100% for Acne, 92% for Psoriasis) for these discrete classes, not continuous numeric outcomes.

datasets_used.name: DermNet
datasets_used.description: Described as the largest independent photo dermatology source, used as a primary source for gathering training data.

use.training: Used to gather training data to train the k-NN algorithm.

datasets_used.name: Doctor's documented skin disease images
datasets_used.description: Images of skin diseases documented by doctors, used alongside DermNet images for training.

use.training: Used to gather training data to train the k-NN algorithm.

datasets_used.name: Patient skin lesion images (Experimental Cohort)
datasets_used.description: Images of actual patient skin lesions captured via USB camera for testing the system's accuracy.

use.testing: Used for actual testing of the device's classification accuracy (25 trials per disease).

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Standard Error of the Mean (SE Mean)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To verify the accuracy of the device by determining if there is a significant difference between the actual data and the expected mean, serving as an accuracy test.
To estimate the range of values for the mean of the GLCM features (Contrast and Energy) for Acne and Psoriasis at a 95% confidence level.
To determine the criteria for accepting or rejecting the null hypothesis in the T-Test analysis.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference between the actual data and the expected mean (test mean of 0.8 for Acne and 4 for Psoriasis).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference between the actual data and the expected mean.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The null hypothesis was accepted for both Acne (p-value 0.149575 > 0.05) and Psoriasis (p-value 0.122596 > 0.05), indicating no significant difference between the actual GLCM feature values and the expected means, thereby proving the accuracy of the device.

Patient Skin Lesion Images (Experimental.notes: The system was tested on 25 trials for Acne and 25 trials for Psoriasis. The null hypothesis was accepted for both diseases via 1-sample T-Test (alpha=0.05), indicating no significant difference between actual data and expected mean, thus proving accuracy. For Acne, p-value (one-sided) was 0.149575. For Psoriasis, p-value (one-sided) was 0.122596.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Gray Level Co-Occurrence Matrix (GLCM) with Wavelet Decomposition for normalization

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion to grayscale (RGB to gray scale)
Normalization using Wavelet Decomposition to remove unwanted noise
Quality control to ensure capture of skin lesions with no unnecessary background
conclusions: The proposed skin disease identification system using a Raspberry Pi, USB camera, GLCM feature extraction (Contrast and Energy), and k-NN classifier is effective for classifying Acne and Psoriasis.
The system achieved 100% accuracy for Acne (25 out of 25 trials) and 92% accuracy for Psoriasis (23 out of 25 trials).
The device enables accessible diagnosis of existing skin diseases without the need for laboratory screenings, addressing challenges in regions with limited medical professional availability.
The use of Wavelet Decomposition for normalization and GLCM for texture analysis proved sufficient for distinguishing between Acne and Psoriasis in this prototype.
The system demonstrates potential for early detection and awareness of skin disease risks in low to medium-income countries where medical professionals are not readily available.
important_remarks: The study is limited to only two skin diseases (Acne and Psoriasis), restricting its generalizability to other dermatological conditions.
The dataset size is very small, with only 25 trials per disease for testing, which limits the statistical power and robustness of the accuracy claims.
Training data sources (DermNet and doctor-documented images) are not quantified in terms of sample size or class distribution, raising concerns about potential data leakage or imbalance not reported.
The system is highly sensitive to image quality; the authors note that unnecessary background or movement causes inconsistencies, indicating a lack of robustness in real-world deployment scenarios.
The study does not address the classification of non-skin images (e.g., objects), meaning the system may produce false positives if used on non-lesion areas.
The hardware prototype relies on a USB camera and Raspberry Pi, which may introduce variability in image capture conditions (lighting, focus) compared to controlled clinical settings.
The k-value for the k-NN classifier is not specified, which is a critical hyperparameter for reproducibility.
The accuracy metrics (100% for Acne, 92% for Psoriasis) are based on a single small-scale prototype test rather than a large-scale, multi-center clinical validation.
